name: Regional Satraps
description: Regional military and court elites mediate Indo-Scythian royal authority.
note: The nobles estate is a technical proxy for heterogeneous regional satrapal and military elites, not a uniform feudal order.

name: Hellenic City Elites
description: Urban elites sustain the last Indo-Greek royal court in the eastern Punjab.
note: The burghers estate is a technical proxy for urban and courtly elites and does not classify the late Indo-Greek polity as a medieval town republic.

name: Clan Assembly
description: Leading warrior clans participate in the council that governs the ganasangha.
note: The nobles estate is a technical proxy for the leading clans and does not equate a ganasangha with a European hereditary nobility.

name: Monastic Patronage
description: Royal endowments sustain Theravada monastic communities and irrigation works.
note: The clergy estate is a technical proxy for Buddhist monastic institutions and not a reconstruction of their exact fiscal or legal status.

name: Senatorial Land Exemption
description: Senatorial landed wealth receives protected fiscal treatment.
note: The nobles estate is a technical proxy for the senatorial aristocracy pending country-specific estate support.

name: Annona
description: Public grain provision stabilizes Rome's urban population.
note: The burghers estate is a technical proxy for the urban plebs receiving the annona.

name: Praetorian Donatives
description: Regular donatives bind the imperial military household to the princeps.
note: The nobles estate is the documented technical fallback: the local estate registry has no safe country-specific attachment point; M10 owns the later legionary crisis interactions.

name: Palace Bureau
description: Palace personnel and clerks provide a court-centred administrative channel.
note: The burghers estate is a technical proxy for a court-bureaucratic actor rather than a claim that eunuchs were burghers.

name: Great-House Autonomy
description: The great Iranian houses retain a substantial political role beside the Arsacid court.
note: The nobles estate represents the Suren and Karen type of great-house politics.

name: Client Royal Autonomy
description: A local royal court retains room to govern within an imperial patronage relationship.
note: The nobles estate is a technical proxy for the court elite of a Roman client kingdom.

name: Kushite Royal Court
description: A royal court sustains the authority of the Kushite co-rulers.
note: The nobles estate is a technical proxy for the court and temple-connected elite rather than a claim of a European-style nobility.

name: Clan Autonomy
description: The constituent clans retain decisive influence within the Xiongnu confederation.
note: The tribes estate directly represents constituent confederation groups rather than an invented settled aristocracy.

name: Early Korean Royal Court
description: Royal authority in Goguryeo is supported by its leading political houses.
note: The nobles estate is a technical court-elite proxy pending a dedicated early Korean institutional pass.

name: Elder Council
description: Leading kin groups constrain and sustain the ruler of a tribal kingdom.
note: The tribes estate is a technical representation of the leading kin groups rather than a complete reconstruction of Germanic or Brittonic assembly practice.

name: Sub-King Autonomy
description: A regional Iranian court retains substantial authority within the Arsacid political world.
note: The nobles estate is a technical regional-court proxy for Media Atropatene and must not be read as a uniform satrapal constitution.

name: Border-Court Autonomy
description: A frontier royal court balances local elites against stronger neighbouring powers.
note: The nobles estate is a technical proxy for a frontier court rather than a claim that Armenia and Osroene shared an institution.

name: Second Temple Priesthood
description: The Jerusalem priesthood retains religious and civic authority around the Temple.
note: The clergy estate is a technical proxy for the Temple priesthood and is not a claim of a Christian clerical hierarchy.

name: Regional Royal Court
description: Leading court figures support the authority of a regional monarch.
note: The nobles estate is a generic technical court-elite proxy and does not reconstruct a uniform ancient aristocracy.

name: Chiefly Court
description: Leading kin and local authorities sustain a developing chiefly polity.
note: The tribes estate is a technical proxy for chiefly authority and does not reconstruct an exact Aksumite political hierarchy.

name: Town-Cluster Council
description: Leading urban households coordinate the public life of a settled town cluster.
note: The burghers estate is a technical urban-community proxy and does not claim a documented Djenné-Djenno council constitution.
